items.foco: Definition and diagnostic criteria of SIBO
items.title: Definition and diagnostic criteria
items.contentHtml:
Definition and diagnostic criteria of SIBO

  • SIBO is defined as an abnormal increase (>10³ CFU/mL) of colonic bacteria in the small intestine.
  • The reference standard is the quantitative culture of jejunal aspirate, with a cut-off point of ≥10³ CFU/mL for aerobic/anaerobic bacteria.
  • The Lactulose hydrogen breath test is the most used non-invasive alternative; an increase of ≥20 ppm in H₂ over baseline within the first 90 minutes suggests SIBO.
  • At least two aspirate samples with ≥10³ CFU/mL are required for definitive diagnosis in investigation.

items.title: Lactulose hydrogen breath test
items.contentHtml:
Lactulose expiratory hydrogen test

  • 10-15 g of lactulose are administered in 250 mL of water; H₂ and CH₄ are measured every 20 minutes for 3 hours.
  • A peak of ≥20 ppm H₂ above baseline within the first 90 minutes indicates SIBO (not to be confused with late colonic peak).
  • Methane production (CH₄ ≥10 ppm) suggests overgrowth of Methanobrevibacter smithii associated with constipation.
  • False positives: rapid transit (gastroparesis), hypochlorhydria, high fiber diet the previous day.

items.title: Hypochlorhydria and motility
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors: hypochlorhydria and motility

  • Hypochlorhydria (gastric pH >4) due to PPI, atrophic gastritis or vagotomy eliminates the acid barrier, allowing ascending colonization.
  • Altered intestinal motility (ineffective migratory motor complex) is the most important factor in scleroderma, diabetes and pseudo-obstruction.
  • Chronic use of PPI increases the risk of SIBO up to 2-3 times in observational studies.
  • Surgeries that alter motility (gastric bypass, resections) favor stasis and overgrowth.

items.title: Classic symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Classic symptoms: bloating and diarrhea

  • The classic triad is abdominal distention, watery diarrhea and flatulence, often postprandial.
  • Diarrhea is due to unabsorbed short-chain fatty acids (osmotic effect) and deconjugated bile salts (secretory effect).
  • Colic-like abdominal pain is common, but non-specific; weight loss and steatorrhea indicate severe forms.
  • SIBO with a predominance of methane is associated with constipation and bloating, not diarrhea.

items.title: Rifaximin first line
items.contentHtml:
Treatment with rifaximin as first line

  • Rifaximin is the first-line drug: 550 mg three times a day for 14 days (dose for SIBO).
  • It is a non-absorbable antibiotic (<0.4% bioavailability) that acts in the intestinal lumen against gram+, gram- and anaerobes.
  • Eradication rate initial ~60-70%; In case of failure, the cycle can be repeated or combined metronidazole (250 mg TID) or ciprofloxacin (500 mg BID) for 7-14 days.
  • In methane-producing SIBO, neomycin (500 mg BID) or metronidazole is added to rifaximin.

items.foco: Difference between SIBO and irritable bowel syndrome
items.title: SIBO vs IBS
items.contentHtml:
Difference between SIBO and irritable bowel syndrome

  • SIBO presents with malabsorption (B12 deficiency, steatorrhea) and responds to antibiotics; IBS is a functional disorder without an objective marker.
  • Up to 30-50% of patients with IBS have associated SIBO (especially diarrhea subtype), but not all SIBO are IBS.
  • The positive breath hydrogen test differentiates SIBO from pure IBS; However, IBS can cause false positives due to rapid transit.
  • Treatment with rifaximin improves symptoms in IBS-D (TARGET study), but does not eradicate the underlying functional cause.

items.title: SIBO and bariatric surgery
items.contentHtml:
SIBO associated with bariatric surgery

  • The Roux-en-Y gastric bypass is the procedure with the highest risk of SIBO due to duodenal exclusion, stasis and change in pH.
  • The prevalence of post-bypass SIBO can reach 40-50% at 2 years, manifesting with distension, diarrhea and B12 deficiency.
  • The Vertical gastrectomy (gastric sleeve) has a lower incidence, but is also associated with hypochlorhydria due to resection of parietal cells.
  • Treatment is similar to that of primary SIBO; Recurrence is frequent and may require prokinetics.

items.foco: Use of prokinetics in recurrent SIBO
items.title: Prokinetics in recurrent SIBO
items.contentHtml:
Use of prokinetics in recurrent SIBO

  • In recurrent SIBO (>3 episodes/year), prokinetics such as prucalopride (1-4 mg/day) or metoclopramide (10 mg TID) are considered to restore the migratory motor complex.
  • prucalopride (5-HT4 agonist) improves intestinal motility without extrapyramidal effects, with evidence in scleroderma and pseudo-obstruction.
  • Erythromycin in subantibiotic doses (50-100 mg/day) acts as a motilin agonist, but its long-term effectiveness is limited by tachyphylaxis.
  • Prokinetics are not first line; They are reserved for cases with documented motility disorder (manometry or scintigraphy).

items.foco: Vitamin B12 deficiency due to SIBO
items.title: B12 deficiency due to SIBO
items.contentHtml:
Vitamin B12 deficiency due to SIBO

  • Anaerobic bacteria in SIBO consume vitamin B12 in the ileum, competing with intrinsic factor, causing deficiency.
  • B12 deficiency produces megaloblastic anemia and peripheral neuropathy; serum B12 levels are usually low with normal or elevated folate.
  • Unlike deficiency due to pernicious anemia, in SIBO the Schilling test can be normal (if corrected with antibiotics).
  • Treatment includes parenteral B12 replacement (1000 µg IM/week) along with antibiotics to eradicate SIBO.

items.foco: SIBO in diseases such as scleroderma
items.title: SIBO in scleroderma
items.contentHtml:
SIBO in diseases such as scleroderma

  • Scleroderma (systemic sclerosis) causes fibrosis and atrophy of the muscularis propria, altering the migratory motor complex.
  • The prevalence of SIBO in scleroderma is >50%, especially in diffuse forms and with anti-Scl-70 antibodies.
  • The clinical features include intestinal pseudo-obstruction, severe distension and malabsorption; The diagnosis is confirmed with a breath hydrogen test.
  • Treatment combines cyclic rifaximin and prokinetics (prucalopride) chronically, with nutritional support.

items.foco: Role of the low FODMAP diet
items.title: Low FODMAP diet
items.contentHtml:
Role of the low-FODMAP diet

  • The low-FODMAP diet reduces the fermentation of short-chain carbohydrates by intestinal bacteria, relieving bloating and flatulence.
  • It does not eradicate SIBO, but it is a symptomatic adjuvant; it is recommended for 4-6 weeks as part of overall management.
  • Foods rich in FODMAPs (lactose, fructose, sorbitol, fructans) are substrates for overgrowth bacteria.
  • After the restriction phase, progressive reintroduction is performed to identify individual tolerances; It is not a diet for life.

items.title: Iodine-sorbent test
items.contentHtml:
Iodine-sorbent test as an alternative

  • The iodine-sorbent test (D-xylose test or labeled ursodeoxycholic acid test) evaluates intestinal absorption; it is not specific for SIBO.
  • The D-xylose test (25 g orally, measurement in blood/urine at 1-2 h) is abnormal in SIBO due to malabsorption, but its sensitivity is low (~50%).
  • The 14C-D-xylose test (with expired CO₂) has better performance, but is not available in most centers.
  • In practice, the breathed hydrogen test remains the preferred non-invasive alternative; The iodine-sorbent test is relegated to research contexts.
